tools.name: convert
tools.description: Convert between different measurement units. Supports length, weight, temperature, area, volume, speed, time, and data units. Use this for any unit conversion between metric, imperial, and digital units. For listing available units or seeing categories, use list_units, categories, or help instead.

tools.name: list_units
tools.description: List all available units in a specific category. Use this to see which units are supported for conversion in a given category. For performing actual conversions, use convert instead.

category.description: The category to list units for (e.g., 'length', 'weight', 'temperature', 'area', 'volume', 'speed', 'time', 'data')

tools.name: categories
tools.description: Show all supported unit conversion categories. Use this to discover what types of conversions are available. For listing units within a specific category, use list_units instead.

tools.name: help
tools.description: Show detailed help and information for a specific unit category. Use this to learn about the units and conversion formulas available for a category. For listing all categories, use categories instead.

category.description: The category to show help for (e.g., 'length', 'weight', 'temperature', 'area', 'volume', 'speed', 'time', 'data')

messages.content: Hi, I'm working on some transportation infrastructure planning and need to compare standards across different countries. I know unit systems vary quite a bit internationally - metric vs imperial, different speed limits, road measurements, that sort of thing. Could you help me understand what unit conversion categories are available for transportation-related calculations? I want to make sure I'm looking at the right options before diving into specifics.
I'd be happy to help you explore what's available for transportation-related unit conversions! Let me show you all the unit categories that are supported.
{"status": "success", "categories": ["length", "weight", "temperature", "area", "volume", "speed", "time", "data"]}
messages.name: skill-tools-categories

messages.content:
Great question! For transportation infrastructure planning, you'll find several useful categories available. Here's what's supported and how each relates to your work:

**Most Directly Relevant:**

- **Length** — Road distances, lane widths, bridge/tunnel dimensions, vehicle measurements (kilometers vs miles, meters vs feet)
- **Speed** — Speed limits and design standards (km/h vs mph)
- **Weight** — Vehicle weight limits, bridge load capacities, cargo restrictions

**Also Useful:**

- **Temperature** — Road maintenance thresholds (freezing conditions), climate considerations for infrastructure design
- **Area** — Parking facilities, right-of-way calculations
- **Volume** — Fuel tank capacities, traffic flow volumes
- **Time** — Travel time calculations, signal timing
- **Data** — For any digital transportation data systems

The length and speed categories will likely be your most frequently used ones for international standard comparisons, since those are where the metric/imperial differences are most pronounced (e.g., converting UK speed limits in mph to km/h for comparison with EU standards, or converting road width specifications).

Would you like me to show you the specific units available within any of these categories—particularly length or speed, since those are probably most relevant to your comparison work?

Yes, I'd particularly like to see what speed units are available. When comparing international speed limits—like US mph versus European km/h—I want to make sure I understand all the options for accurate conversions. Could you show me the specific speed units supported?

{"status": "success", "category": "speed", "units": [{"name": "meters per second", "symbol": "m/s", "type": "metric"}, {"name": "kilometers per hour", "symbol": "km/h", "type": "metric"}, {"name": "miles per hour", "symbol": "mph", "type": "imperial"}, {"name": "knots", "symbol": "kn", "type": "nautical"}, {"name": "feet per second", "symbol": "ft/s", "type": "imperial"}, {"name": "mach", "symbol": "M", "type": "ratio"}, {"name": "speed of light", "symbol": "c", "type": "constant"}]}
messages.name: skill-tools-list_units

messages.content:
Here's the complete list of speed units available:

| Unit | Symbol | Type |
|------|--------|------|
| Meters per second | m/s | Metric |
| Kilometers per hour | km/h | Metric |
| Miles per hour | mph | Imperial |
| Knots | kn | Nautical |
| Feet per second | ft/s | Imperial |
| Mach | M | Ratio |
| Speed of light | c | Constant |

**For your international speed limit comparisons**, you'll primarily be working with:

- **km/h** — Used throughout Europe, most of the world
- **mph** — Used in US, UK, and a few others

These two are the key ones for your use case. You can easily convert between them—for example, a US highway limit of 65 mph equals approximately 105 km/h, while a typical European motorway limit of 120 km/h equals about 75 mph.

**Other notes:**

- **Knots** — Useful if you're comparing any maritime or aviation transportation standards
- **m/s** — Common in scientific contexts and some engineering specifications
- **ft/s** — Occasionally used in US construction/engineering specs
- **Mach/speed of light** — Not relevant for typical road infrastructure but available for reference

Would you like me to run any specific conversions for you, such as comparing specific speed limits across countries?

Perfect, that's exactly what I need. Could you convert some common US speed limits to km/h? I'm thinking of the typical ones we use—say 25 mph for school zones, 55 mph on urban highways, 65 mph on rural interstates, and 75 mph on some rural freeways. I want to see how those compare to European and other international limits for our infrastructure standards report.
{"status": "success", "tool": "convert", "original_value": 25.0, "from_unit": "mph", "to_unit": "km/h", "result": 40.2335}
messages.name: skill-tools-convert

messages.content: {"status": "success", "tool": "convert", "original_value": 55.0, "from_unit": "mph", "to_unit": "km/h", "result": 88.5137}
messages.name: skill-tools-convert

messages.content:
{"raw": "should contain:
- status: "success"
- tool: "convert"
- result or converted value

<STATE> should update:
- last_tool: "convert" (same)
- last_tool_arguments: {"value": 65.0, "from_unit": "mph", "to_unit": "km/h"}
- last_tool_result_summary: "65 mph = 104.6071 km/h" (or similar precision)

Let me be precise with the calculation:
65 * 1.609344 = 104.60736 km/h

I'll use a reasonable precision.
</think>

<RESPONSE>
{"status": "success", "tool": "convert", "value": 65.0, "from_unit": "mph", "to_unit": "km/h", "result": 104.60736}", "parse_error": true}

messages.name: skill-tools-convert

messages.content:
Here's the conversion of those common US speed limits to km/h:

| US Speed Limit | Converted to km/h |
|----------------|-------------------|
| 25 mph (school zones) | ~40 km/h |
| 55 mph (urban highways) | ~89 km/h |
| 65 mph (rural interstates) | ~105 km/h |
| 75 mph (rural freeways) | ~121 km/h |

**For context with international standards:**

- **European Union**: Typical motorway limits are 130 km/h, with some countries (e.g., Germany on sections of the Autobahn) having no speed limit. Urban speeds are typically 50 km/h.
- **UK**: Motorways are 70 mph (~113 km/h), with 30 mph in built-up areas.
- **Canada**: Varies by province; typically 100-110 km/h on highways.
- **Australia**: Generally 100-110 km/h, with some rural highways at 130 km/h.

So your US rural interstate limit of 65 mph (~105 km/h) is notably lower than the EU standard of 130 km/h, but closer to limits in Canada and Australia. The US urban highway limit of 55 mph (~89 km/h) is similar to many European urban expressways.

Would you like me to convert any other speed limits, or perhaps look at length conversions for road width comparisons?

That would actually be helpful. Beyond speed limits, I also need to convert various distance measurements for our infrastructure planning—things like lane widths, shoulder dimensions, and right-of-way specifications that we sometimes receive in different unit systems from international partners. Could you show me what length units are available, and then help me convert some typical road measurements? For example, standard US lane widths are around 12 feet, and I'd like to compare those to metric specifications used in Europe and elsewhere.
{"status": "success", "tool": "list_units", "category": "length", "units": ["mm", "cm", "m", "km", "in", "ft", "yd", "mi", "nm"]}
messages.name: skill-tools-list_units

messages.content:
Here's the complete list of length units available:

| Unit | Symbol | Type |
|------|--------|------|
| Millimeters | mm | Metric |
| Centimeters | cm | Metric |
| Meters | m | Metric |
| Kilometers | km | Metric |
| Inches | in | Imperial |
| Feet | ft | Imperial |
| Yards | yd | Imperial |
| Miles | mi | Imperial |
| Nautical miles | nm | Nautical |

Now let me convert that 12-foot standard US lane width to metric for you:
{"status": "success", "tool": "convert", "result": "12 ft = 3.6576 m", "value": 12.0, "from_unit": "ft", "to_unit": "m", "converted_value": 3.6576}
messages.name: skill-tools-convert

messages.content:
Here's your conversion:

| US Measurement | Converted to Metric |
|----------------|---------------------|
| **12 ft (standard lane width)** | **3.66 m** |

That 12-foot lane width equals about **3.66 meters**—very close to the European standard of 3.5-3.75 meters for motorway lanes.

Would you like me to convert some other common road infrastructure measurements? I can do things like:

- Typical shoulder widths (6-10 ft)
- Vehicle clearance heights (standard 13.5-14 ft for commercial vehicles)
- Right-of-way specifications
- Bridge clearance heights
- Parking space dimensions

Just let me know what specific measurements you're working with, or if you'd like me to run conversions for a few common ones to build out a reference table for your report.

That would be great for building out a reference table. While we're on the subject of infrastructure measurements, I should also look into weight units—we frequently deal with vehicle weight limits, bridge load capacities, and cargo restrictions that come from different countries using different systems. Could you show me what weight units are available? I need to compare US weight limits (often in pounds or tons) to the metric specifications used in Europe and other regions for our regulatory compliance work.

{"raw": "` block with valid JSON showing the available weight units
2. Create a `<STATE>` block updating the state with the new tool call

Let me construct the response:

```json
{
  "status": "success",

  "category": "weight",
  "units": [
    "kg",
    "g",
    "mg",
    "lb",

    "category": "weight"
  },
  "last_tool_result_summary": "Listed 8 weight units",
  "last_tool_result": {
    "category": "weight",
    "units": ["kg", "g", "mg", "lb", "oz", "t", "st", "μg"]
  }
}
```

Wait, I need to preserve the existing state fields. 

I'll carefully merge the new tool call information with the existing state, ensuring I maintain the previous conversion details while adding the new list_units information. The key is to update the state minimally, preserving the context of previous interactions while incorporating the new tool call's specifics.

The state update will include the new tool name, its arguments, and a summary of the result, while keeping the previous conversion information intact. This approach ensures a clean, incremental update to the session state.
</think>

<RESPONSE>
{"status": "success", "tool": "list_units", "category": "weight", "units": ["kg", "g", "mg", "lb", "oz", "t", "st", "μg"]}", "parse_error": true}
messages.name: skill-tools-list_units

messages.content:
Here's the list of weight units available:

| Unit | Symbol | Type |
|------|--------|------|
| Kilograms | kg | Metric |
| Grams | g | Metric |
| Milligrams | mg | Metric |
| Pounds | lb | Imperial |
| Ounces | oz | Imperial |
| Metric tons | t | Metric |
| Short tons | st | Imperial |
| Micrograms | μg | Metric |

**For your regulatory compliance work**, the key conversions will be:

- **kg ↔ lb** — Everyday vehicle weights, cargo limits
- **t ↔ st (short tons)** — Bridge load limits, heavy vehicle restrictions

This is an important distinction: US "tons" are typically **short tons** (2,000 lb = 907 kg), while international standards use **metric tons** (1,000 kg = 2,205 lb). That's a significant difference of about 10%!

For example:
- US interstate weight limit: 80,000 lb ≈ 36,000 kg ≈ 36 t
- European general weight limit: 40-44 t (varies by country)
- Typical European axle load limit: 10-12 t

Would you like me to run some specific conversions for common US weight limits to metric? I can do things like:

- Convert 80,000 lb (US federal truck weight limit) to kg and metric tons
- Convert typical axle load limits (20,000 lb per axle) to kg
- Convert US bridge load ratings to metric equivalents

Just let me know which specific measurements you need for your reference table!

Yes, those conversions would be incredibly helpful for our reference table. Could you run those conversions I mentioned? I'd like to see:

1. The US federal truck weight limit of 80,000 lb converted to both kg and metric tons
2. Typical US axle load limits of around 20,000 lb per axle converted to kg

And while we're at it, could you also convert some common European limits to imperial so we can compare both directions? For instance, the typical European general weight limit of 40 t and axle load limits of 10-12 t converted to pounds.

This will give us a solid bidirectional reference for our regulatory compliance work.
{"status": "success", "tool": "convert", "value": 80000.0, "from_unit": "lb", "to_unit": "kg", "result": 36287.3896}

messages.content:
Here's the complete bidirectional reference for your regulatory compliance work:

## US to Metric Conversions

| US Specification | Converted Value |
|------------------|-----------------|
| Federal truck weight limit (80,000 lb) | 36,287 kg / 36.3 t |
| Typical axle load limit (20,000 lb) | 9,072 kg |

## Metric to Imperial Conversions

| European Specification | Converted Value |
|------------------------|-----------------|
| General weight limit (40 t) | 88,185 lb |
| Typical axle load (10 t) | 22,046 lb |
| Higher axle load (12 t) | 26,455 lb |

## Key Takeaways for Your Report:

- **80,000 lb = ~36.3 metric tons** — The US federal truck limit is significantly lower than the European 40 t general limit (about 10% lighter)
- **20,000 lb axle = ~9.1 t** — US axle limits are comparable to European 10 t limits, though some EU countries allow up to 12 t per axle
- The difference between short tons (US) and metric tons is crucial: **1 short ton = 0.907 metric tons**

This should give you a solid bidirectional reference table for your infrastructure planning and regulatory compliance documentation. Would you like me to convert any other measurements, or perhaps look at additional unit categories like volume (for fuel capacity comparisons) or temperature (for winter maintenance threshold comparisons)?
